findings.title: Brief gives March 14 incident date contradicted by records showing March 12
findings.description: The location is corroborated, but the date is not: the brief says March 14, 2021, while the medical records and police report identify the workplace accident/incident as March 12, 2021. The medical record also shows admission on March 12 and surgery on March 13, making a March 14 incident impossible.

evidence.quote: This action arises from a workplace incident on March 14, 2021, at a commercial construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Location:          2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, CA 90006

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion states March 14, while both the medical record and police report state March 12 for the same incident at the same location. This is a direct, verbatim date contradiction.

findings.title: Fact 3 gives March 14 accident date contradicted by March 12 records
findings.description: The sources corroborate that Rivera was working on scaffolding and that the structure gave way, but they place the accident on March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021. The March 12 hospital admission and March 13 surgery described in the medical record make the brief's March 14 date incompatible with the source chronology.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 3

evidence.quote: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
DATE OF ADMISSION:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
On March 12, 2021, at approximately 10:45 AM, officers responded to a report of an industrial accident at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, a commercial renovation site.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion’s Fact 3 states March 14, while medical and police records repeatedly identify the same scaffolding accident as occurring on March 12. The hospital admission date independently confirms the March 12 chronology.

findings.title: Brief says Rivera lacked PPE, but police and witness records say he wore required PPE
findings.description: The brief says Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment. Both the police report and eyewitness statement say he was wearing required PPE, including a harness, and explain that the lanyard came free because the anchor point was part of the collapsed scaffold.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 4

evidence.quote: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
Carlos was wearing his hard hat, safety harness, and high-visibility vest, consistent with site PPE requirements.
His harness was still on, but the lanyard had come free â€” the anchor point it was attached to was part of the scaffolding that collapsed.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The contradiction is direct: the motion says Rivera was not wearing required PPE, while both the police report and witness statement say he was wearing a hard hat and harness. The lanyard explanation also directly refutes the implication that he lacked fall-arrest equipment.

findings.title: Time-bar section gives March 14 incident date contradicted by March 12 records
findings.description: The police report and medical history identify the incident as March 12, 2021. The medical follow-up states Rivera underwent surgery for the fracture on March 13, 2021, which could not have happened before a March 14 incident.

evidence.quote: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
He underwent intramedullary nailing of the left tibial shaft fracture on March 13, 2021.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion states March 14 in the limitations argument, while the police report and medical record state March 12. The March 13 surgery date makes a March 14 accident chronologically impossible.

findings.title: Time-barred heading is undercut by the brief’s concession that filing was nominally within the limitations window
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations. Filing on 2023-03-10 is inside the two-year window even using the brief's own March 14, 2021 incident date, making the time-barred framing self-defeating absent a developed accrual argument.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The heading says the claims are time-barred, while the body expressly concedes the filing falls nominally within the limitations window. Confidence is somewhat reduced because the brief gestures at an accrual argument, though the quoted language does not actually develop one that reconciles the heading.

findings.title: Brief says Rivera presented no rebuttal evidence, but file contains unsafe-scaffold evidence
findings.description: The source file includes evidence from Rivera's side, including a witness statement taken by plaintiff's counsel, describing unsafe scaffold conditions and prior notice to Harmon's foreman. That directly conflicts with the assertion that Rivera presented no evidence to rebut reasonable-care/OSHA-compliance framing.
findings.brief_location: III.B. OSHA Compliance Establishes a Presumption of Reasonable Care

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
I noticed several things that concerned me:
The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner. Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
Carlos was doing his job and following safety protocols. The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.
The base plates on the failed section appeared to be resting on uneven ground, with one plate sitting on a loose plywood sheet rather than a stable footing.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion says there is no rebuttal evidence, while the witness statement and police report describe unsafe footing and prior notice to Harmon’s foreman. Confidence is slightly reduced because whether evidence was formally “presented” in the litigation record is not fully shown by the excerpts.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette v. Superior Court appears doctored
findings.description: Privette is a real and well-known California Supreme Court case, but the quoted sentence is materially overbroad. The word “never” and the formulation “when the injuries arise from the contracted work” convert Privette’s limited bar on peculiar-risk/vicarious liability into a categorical immunity for hirers, which is not the doctrine. Known actual language/rule: Privette held that the peculiar-risk doctrine does not permit an injured employee of an independent contractor, covered by workers’ compensation, to recover tort damages from the nonnegligent hirer on a vicarious-liability theory. The Privette line does not eliminate all hirer liability; later cases recognize exceptions such as retained control with affirmative contribution and concealed hazards.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted categorical “never liable” rule conflicts with the canonical Privette doctrine and its recognized exceptions. Confidence is high but capped because the actual Privette text is not supplied in the record being adjudicated.

findings.title: Privette proposition stated as categorical hirer immunity
findings.description: The quoted absolute statement that a hirer is “never liable” is not an accurate statement of California law. Privette establishes a general no-liability/presumptive-delegation rule, but California Supreme Court cases recognize exceptions, including Hooker v. Department of Transportation for retained control that affirmatively contributes to injury, Kinsman v. Unocal for concealed hazards, and McKown v. Wal-Mart for defective equipment supplied by the hirer.

evidence.quote: Id. at 702.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The legal point is strong because Privette’s nonliability rule and its recognized exceptions are canonical in California tort law. The confidence is not higher because this finding’s own evidence only shows a short-form citation rather than the full proposition being challenged; the fuller duplicate is finding-12.

findings.title: Brief’s “Apex, not Harmon” responsibility framing is undercut by evidence of Harmon direction and notice
findings.description: The sources corroborate that Apex was Rivera's employer, but they undercut the brief's framing that Harmon was not involved in scaffolding operations or safety. The police report and witness statement say Harmon's foreman directed the crew to work on the east-side scaffolding and was told about safety concerns before the collapse.
findings.brief_location: III.A. Harmon Is Not Liable Under the Privette Doctrine

evidence.quote: Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaffolding operations and for ensuring its employees followed appropriate safety procedures.
Employer:      Apex Staffing Solutions
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.

Tran further stated that she and other crew members had raised concerns about the condition of the east-side scaffolding earlier in the week, noting that some of the cross-braces "didn't look right" and that the base on the far end "was sitting on a piece of plywood that kept shifting." Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner. Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
I want to note that Donner had personally directed us to work on that section that morning and dismissed my concern about the base plate.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The record supports Apex as employer, but multiple quotes show Harmon’s foreman directed the work and allegedly dismissed safety concerns. The contradiction is partly interpretive because responsibility for employment and responsibility for site direction can coexist.

findings.title: Seabright does not create an OSHA-compliance presumption of due care
findings.description: Seabright supports the Privette/delegation rule for workplace-safety duties, including Cal-OSHA duties. It does not stand for the broader proposition that statutory safety compliance is “highly probative” of due care, and it does not say that an unblemished OSHA inspection record insulates a hirer from tort liability. The brief overstates the effect of compliance and omits the recognized Privette exceptions.

evidence.quote: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).

findings.confidence_reasoning: Seabright is a well-known Privette-line case about delegation of workplace-safety duties, not a broad evidentiary presumption based on OSHA compliance. Confidence is capped because the finding relies on legal characterization of the case rather than a provided excerpt from Seabright.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. appears fabricated
findings.description: The cited Ninth Circuit case and the quoted OSHA-compliance presumption appear suspect. The asserted rule is not a recognized general OSHA/negligence rule: OSHA compliance is generally at most evidence relevant to due care, while OSHA violations may sometimes be evidence of negligence or negligence per se; full compliance does not create a broad rebuttable presumption of due care. The quoted language appears to assert a non-existent rule and is likely not genuine.

evidence.quote: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted presumption is legally suspect and unusually tailored, and the related citation has facial red flags. Confidence remains moderate because no authoritative text or search result is provided proving the quote is fabricated.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: The case and citation appear suspect, including a possible mismatch between 887 F.2d and the stated 1991 date, and the asserted OSHA-compliance presumption is not a familiar California or Ninth Circuit negligence rule. Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision on state-law negligence would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort law.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)

findings.confidence_reasoning: The volume/date mismatch and unfamiliar, highly tailored rule are meaningful red flags, but the record provided here does not include a conclusive search result proving the citation is fabricated. This overlaps with the more complete quotation-focused Kellerman finding.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co.
findings.description: The case appears suspiciously tailored to the brief’s scaffolding/Privette argument, and the verifier reports that it could not recognize or locate the C.D. Cal. authority. Even if real, a federal district court decision would be persuasive at most and not binding on a California Superior Court applying California law.

evidence.quote: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)

findings.confidence_reasoning: The citation is facially specific, but the evidence supplied does not include a database search result or reporter mismatch proving nonexistence. It is kept because the upstream verifier identified concrete suspicion, but the support is substantially inferential.

findings.title: Elapsed-time assertion is contradicted by the record incident date and arithmetic
findings.description: The provided documents do not verify the complaint filing date, but they do contradict the incident date used in the elapsed-time assertion. If the complaint date were March 10, 2023, then from the source-supported incident date of March 12, 2021 to March 10, 2023 is one year and 363 days; even from the brief's March 14, 2021 date, the interval is one year and 361 days, not one year and 362 days.

evidence.quote: Rivera did not file his complaint until March 10, 2023 â€” one year and 362 days after the incident.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The record-supported incident date is March 12, not the date assumed in the brief, and the day-count calculation is mechanically checkable. Confidence is slightly below certain because the complaint filing date itself is only quoted from the motion, not verified by a docket document.

evidence.quote: Under the doctrine of assumption of risk, a plaintiff who voluntarily encounters a known danger cannot recover for injuries arising from that danger.

judicial_memo: The brief contains several serious and well-supported factual contradictions and legal-authority defects. Most notably, it repeatedly states that the workplace incident occurred on March 14, 2021, including in the introduction, Fact 3, and the time-bar section, while the medical records and police report identify March 12, 2021; the medical record also reflects admission on March 12 and surgery on March 13, making the stated March 14 chronology incompatible with the records. The brief further asserts that Rivera lacked required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment, but the police report and eyewitness statement say he wore required PPE, including a harness, and that the lanyard came free because the anchor point was part of the collapsed scaffold. Its “time-barred” framing is undercut by its own concession that filing was nominally within the limitations period. The Privette quotation appears materially overbroad, and the brief states categorical hirer immunity despite recognized exceptions. Two cited authorities, Kellerman and Whitmore, could not be located or verified in available sources.

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that under California law, a hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.quoted_text: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that the Supreme Court held that a hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.full_citation: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that summary judgment is proper to bar claims against a hirer where the subcontractor controlled the scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that full compliance with applicable OSHA standards entitles an employer to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.full_citation: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), is a well-known California Supreme Court decision and the citation details are consistent with that authority.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that a person who hires an independent contractor generally is not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to the contractor’s employees, who are covered by workers’ compensation. Later California cases describe the doctrine as creating a presumption that the hirer delegated workplace-safety responsibilities to the contractor, subject to recognized exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition is broadly consistent with the Privette doctrine, especially as developed in later cases: a hirer is presumptively not liable for on-the-job injuries to an independent contractor’s employees arising from the contracted work. The brief should not omit that the presumption is not absolute; exceptions include retained control affirmatively contributing to injury, concealed hazardous conditions, and furnishing defective equipment, among others.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: This is an id. citation to Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), a real and well-known California Supreme Court case. The pinpoint to page 702 is plausible within the decision.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette generally bars hirer liability to an independent contractor’s employee under the peculiar-risk doctrine because workers’ compensation covers the employee and imposing vicarious liability on the hirer would be inequitable. The Privette line of cases recognizes exceptions to the general rule.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The quoted absolute statement that a hirer is “never liable” is not an accurate statement of California law. Privette establishes a general no-liability/presumptive-delegation rule, but California Supreme Court cases recognize exceptions, including Hooker v. Department of Transportation for retained control that affirmatively contributes to injury, Kinsman v. Unocal for concealed hazards, and McKown v. Wal-Mart for defective equipment supplied by the hirer.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this C.D. Cal. case. Concrete fabrication signals include the highly on-point case name involving a scaffolding company and a parenthetical that fits the brief’s precise scaffolding/Privette argument unusually well. No reporter-range mismatch is provided, but the authority appears suspiciously tailored.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify the existence or holding of this case, I cannot assess whether it supports the asserted proposition.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a C.D. Cal. federal district court decision would be persuasive at most and not binding on a California Superior Court applying California law.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this case, and there are concrete red flags: the quoted OSHA-compliance presumption is suspiciously tailored and not a familiar California or Ninth Circuit negligence rule; additionally, 887 F.2d is generally associated with decisions around 1989 rather than 1991, making the citation details suspect.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify this case, I cannot assess the quoted proposition. Separately, the asserted rule is doubtful as a matter of California law: violations of statutes or safety regulations may support negligence per se, but compliance with OSHA/Cal-OSHA standards does not generally create an insulating presumption that the defendant exercised reasonable care in all respects.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision on state-law negligence would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort law.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), is a real and well-known California Supreme Court Privette-line decision; the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Seabright held that a hirer presumptively delegates to an independent contractor the duty to provide a safe workplace for the contractor’s employees, including duties imposed by Cal-OSHA regulations, and therefore the hirer was not liable to the contractor’s employee for breach of those delegated safety duties.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Seabright supports the Privette/delegation rule for workplace-safety duties, including Cal-OSHA duties. It does not stand for the broader proposition that statutory safety compliance is “highly probative” of due care, and it does not say that an unblemished OSHA inspection record insulates a hirer from tort liability. The brief overstates the effect of compliance and omits the recognized Privette exceptions.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: California Code of Civil Procedure section 335.1 is a real California statute governing limitations periods for personal injury and wrongful-death-type claims.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year limitations period for actions for assault, battery, injury to, or death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect of another.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The statute supports the proposition that California personal-injury claims generally have a two-year limitations period. However, in the brief’s facts, a March 10, 2023 filing for a March 14, 2021 injury appears timely within two years, so the statute does not support the brief’s broader “time-barred” argument absent some separate accrual or limitations theory.
